questions.q: A program repeatedly accesses the same array location within a tight loop. Which principle of memory access explains why this data will likely remain in the cache?
options: Spatial locality—nearby memory addresses are accessed together
Temporal locality—recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again soon
Associativity—multiple copies of data are stored throughout the cache
Write-back policy—the cache holds data until the program finishes
questions.explanation: Temporal locality states that data accessed recently is likely accessed again in the near future. A tight loop repeatedly accessing the same location demonstrates this principle perfectly. Spatial locality applies to sequential accesses; associativity and write-back affect cache organization, not access patterns.

questions.q: In a fully associative cache, an incoming memory address can be placed in any cache line. Why do most modern processors use set-associative caches instead?
options: Fully associative caches are always slower due to required sequential searches
Set-associative caches reduce the complexity and cost of the tag comparison hardware
Fully associative caches cannot implement write-back policies
Set-associative caches eliminate cache misses entirely
questions.explanation: Fully associative requires comparing the incoming tag against all cache lines simultaneously (expensive hardware). Set-associative caches partition the cache into sets, reducing comparisons to a manageable number of ways per set. The other options either misstate the trade-offs or are incorrect.

questions.q: A cache has 8 sets and each set contains 4 lines (ways), with 64-byte blocks. What is the total cache size?
options: 256 bytes
2 KB
256 KB
32 KB
questions.explanation: Cache size = sets × ways × block size = 8 × 4 × 64 = 2048 bytes = 2 KB. This is a direct calculation; the other options result from miscomputing the formula or conflating different metrics.

questions.q: When a write to a cache line occurs in a write-back cache, the data is first written to the cache. When is the data written to main memory?
options: Immediately after the cache write completes
Only when the line is evicted from the cache and marked dirty
Whenever a read miss occurs in another cache
At fixed intervals regardless of cache state
questions.explanation: Write-back caches delay memory updates until a dirty line must be evicted, reducing memory traffic. Write-through would write immediately; the other options misrepresent write-back behavior. This design trades memory consistency complexity for bandwidth savings.

questions.q: A program has a cache miss rate of 5% and each miss costs 200 cycles to fetch from main memory. Cache hits take 1 cycle. What is the average memory access time?
options: 10.5 cycles
1.95 cycles
11 cycles
105 cycles
questions.explanation: Average time = hit time × hit rate + miss time × miss rate = 1 × 0.95 + 200 × 0.05 = 0.95 + 10 = 10.95 ≈ 11 cycles. This formula accounts for both the fast path (cache hits) and the slow path (misses).

questions.q: In an L1 cache with 64 KB capacity and 64-byte blocks, how many sets exist if the cache is 4-way set-associative?
questions.explanation: Number of sets = (cache size) / (block size × associativity) = 64 KB / (64 bytes × 4) = 65536 / 256 = 256 sets. This follows directly from the cache organization equation.

questions.q: When a cache miss occurs and the requested block must be loaded from memory, what happens to the evicted line if the cache uses a write-back policy and the evicted line is dirty?
options: The dirty line is immediately discarded and lost
The dirty line is written to memory before the new block is loaded
The new block is loaded before the dirty line is flushed, causing a pipeline stall
The write-back policy does not apply to evictions
questions.explanation: Write-back caches must write dirty evicted lines to memory to preserve data correctness. This must complete before loading the new block. Discarding would cause data loss; loading first would overwrite the dirty data.

questions.q: A processor uses a hierarchical cache with L1 (32 KB, 4-cycle hit latency) and L2 (256 KB, 12-cycle hit latency). If L1 has a 10% miss rate and the L2 miss rate is 5%, what is the effective memory access time (L3/main memory = 100 cycles)?
options: 1.7 cycles
5.6 cycles
8.4 cycles
12.1 cycles
questions.explanation: EMAT = 4 + 0.10×(12 + 0.05×100) = 4 + 0.10×17 = 4 + 1.7 = 5.7 ≈ 5.6 cycles. Each miss in L1 incurs the L2 access time, and each L2 miss adds the L3 penalty.

questions.q: Which memory access pattern would exploit spatial locality to maximize cache effectiveness?
options: Randomly jumping between unrelated memory addresses throughout the program
Sequentially reading array elements one after another from memory
Frequently re-accessing the same single memory location
Reading only the most recently written data
questions.explanation: Spatial locality means accessing nearby addresses in quick succession. Sequential array access ensures that when a cache block is loaded, the next several accesses are already in that block. Random access and repeated single-address patterns exploit temporal, not spatial, locality.

questions.q: In a write-through cache, every write operation updates both the cache and main memory immediately. What is a major drawback of this approach compared to write-back?
options: Write-through caches cannot detect dirty lines
Write-through generates much higher memory traffic, reducing available bandwidth for reads
Write-through provides weaker data consistency guarantees
Write-through requires more transistors to implement
questions.explanation: Write-through writes to memory on every store, consuming bandwidth and increasing average memory access time. Write-back batches writes until eviction, reducing traffic. The other options either mischaracterize write-through or contradict cache design realities.
